items.myth: Seaborn replaces Matplotlib—you never need Matplotlib again.
items.reality: Seaborn generates Matplotlib figures; you still use `plt` for show/save, subplots, and fine-grained tweaks. Seaborn covers statistical patterns, not every custom diagram.
items.example: After `sns.heatmap(...)`, call `plt.tight_layout()` and `fig.savefig('corr.png')`— both are Matplotlib operations on the figure Seaborn created.

items.myth: A dark red cell in a correlation heatmap means one feature causes another.
items.reality: Heatmaps display Pearson (or Spearman) correlation coefficients— symmetric measures of association. Causation requires separate evidence.
items.example: Height and weight may correlate at r=0.7 in a dataset; the heatmap shows association, not that gaining weight makes you taller.

items.myth: pairplot always proves which features are important for prediction.
items.reality: Pair plots show bivariate visual patterns; importance depends on the model, interactions, and metrics like AUC—not diagonal separation alone.
items.example: Two classes may overlap on every pairplot axis yet separate well in 10D after a nonlinear model— visual EDA is necessary but not sufficient.
